title: Traveling with Kyle - Guatemala Part 2/2, El Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua

transcript.text: Okay, so yeah, I went to Copal with Stasia, my American friend from Santa Cruz, to Copal
And stayed there like two nights and spent a day. Copal was a cool Mayan ruin
Which is actually in Honduras right across the border. Lots of you know
Pyramids and like like little square areas. It looked like it was designed as a
School to me. And then took the bus back, it was kind of a long trip, but then I
Spent a couple of days in one night with Stasia, what's it? Antigua. Then she
Went back to I guess go to some like hippie party, a rainbow gathering or
Something and it was in the lake, but I want to spend another day in the city
Which is beautiful. So I guess it was a capital at the beginning with cobblestone
Roads and it's really touristy. Then I went back and I stayed there like maybe
Another month or something. And, and then left there with my bike with my buddy Glyn and
But we partied a couple days in Gua, Gringolandia or whatever that town is called in the
Northeast edge of the lake. Oh and but while I was in staying in San Pedro at one point I was actually on
Acid on my porch. I had a nice house and nice front yard, all these trees on it. I was
Sitting on my porch and I felt a couple days beforehand these there the the bus
Drivers drive like crazy and in Guatemala. We actually took a bus, me, Glyn, and
Another Swedish kid to climb the highest mountain in Guatemala. We took the bus up and then got off the
Bus and then just hiked up to this highest mountain. It was like 13,000 feet. It was above
Timberline. It's beautiful. And then came down and stayed one night in a hotel and then took the bus back.
But the bus was driving insanely fast on the side of the mountain. So fast. It was like 60 miles an hour. So
Fast it was actually, the wheels were actually screeching as they were going on our corner.
And yeah, while I was in Guatemala these two buses crashed into each other that were driving
Around the lake. And I think everybody died in both of the buses. And one of the buses was carrying
People who were from San Pedro, the Mayans. And we were hanging out at the lakeside, you know,
Restaurant place. And they carried all the bodies over in the boats, like 50 bodies. And the next day
I was, uh, I was on my porch on acid and I felt this, I felt a, a, um, tremor. And then right then I
Heard, started hearing like wailing. And that's when they had the big funeral procession up on the top.
Um, tripping. And I was riding, I rode my bike. It was cool having a bike there because I ride it
Everywhere. Instead of walking, I could get from one side of the town to the neither. And the roads were really
Steep right up from where the road where I live. So anyway, I left with my friends and we stayed
There for like three days, just drinking and partying. And then we took the bus and it's easy
To take a bus with a bike in any, in any country I've ever been to, especially in several countries,
Because you just throw the bike on the top of the bus. And we went down to, uh, um, I guess into the
Next town next, uh, what was it? Uh, the next, uh, country to the south, um, El Salvador and checked
Out a couple of towns and stayed in one town on North, I remember. And then they decided to go
Climb a couple of volcanoes. And for some reason, I didn't want to climb the volcanoes with them. I
Just wanted to ride my bike down to the capital city. And, uh, and then I tried to ride, and then I
Rode my bike down to, uh, the, uh, the western side to this beach to do some surfing. It was supposed
To be some surf place. Oh, when we were in Gringo land or whatever, there was another, uh, um, tremor
That we could felt that lasted like a minute. And I guess it killed a bunch of people anywhere. Then I
Rode down and, uh, uh, but there were no waves, so I didn't surf. I stayed in a really interesting hotel,
Though. I had, it was like this room on the top higher than all the other rooms in this hotel,
Like a tower. I looked all around. I was like the highest building in the whole town. And when I was
Staying there, there was another, uh, earthquake that I felt. But then I took the bus up again,
Because it was so steep. Probably should have ridden it, but I took the bus up. And then I tried to
Ride my bike into, uh, Honduras. But, um, um, I, I rode all the way into Honduras and they wouldn't
Let me in because my passport was totally smudge. You couldn't see my face at all. I shouldn't know
And they wouldn't let me in. I guess it got kind of wet when I started stirring it. And there was one
Of those, like an old passport they gave me in Indonesia that it wasn't laminated at all. It was
Just a piece. It was just my picture glued onto the passport. So obviously it was going to get wet
And smudge. So I took it back to the, uh, um, place and I saw my friends again there, kind of bumped
Into them. They're staying right next to me. But I went back to the, uh, uh, the embassy and I got
Another passport. Um, and they gave it to me that day. It's pretty cool. Um, and then, but instead of
Riding my bike into Honduras, I was, I didn't like want to ride the same trail again. So I took the
Bus and then I took the bus actually all the way across Honduras. But I stayed a couple of places
Along the way, a couple of towns and checked them out. And it was a, uh, first town was pretty. Um,
Then I stayed in the capital of Honduras for a couple of days and it was, uh, kind of a gloomy place.
I mean, it was hilly and stuff, but not a lot of trees. And they did have a hill in the center, but
The Hondurians were, I guess, the grumpiest, saddest bunch of people I've ever seen.
And, uh, the ladies, um, in my hotel stole a bunch of my stuff. But, uh, then I took a bus into,
Uh, what was it called? The island on the east side of Honduras? Oh man, Roatan.
And I hung out in Roatan for like a week. I did the scuba class, I guess scuba two,
Because I did scuba one when I was in Thailand. Um, that was cool. We went off these cliffs and
Went way down and we did a wreck dive. And it was a cool place because it was like this big party place,
Lots of, uh, gringos there partying outside. And, um, then I, uh, took the, I rode,
And I rode across Roatan. Then I took the bus back to the capital and then back across Honduras into,
Uh, Nicaragua. Um, and took the bus all the way to, uh, um, that, the capital. I can't remember what
It's called, but it's right on the south side of Lake Nicaragua, which is a big lake, but it was
Like, it had kind of a weird color to it. Um, and that was an interesting town. It was a very, uh,
Uh, I guess they had in the eighties, they had a dictator.
She,
